hard.next_step: 5/27 - RFP deadline extended to June 26th from June 5th per email from Chris Chovin. Working with Ritika, Jordan and Vishal on submission with weekly check ins. 5/12MF - RFP received, need to submit intent to respond by 5/20 with submission due June 5th.
hard.ais_why: April 23 call (Chris Chovin) described a blank-slate S2C need to replace outdated Excel/Outlook tools, with heavy data-security and slow-adoption constraints from French ownership and nuclear regulations; zero AI/Merlin mentions across the conversation.

north_star_verdict.headline: We are answering Orano's formal S2P RFP (submission due 17 Jul) with no champion, no economic buyer, and a single procurement coordinator as our only contact. The RFP is genuinely live, but with zero relationship inside the committee this is a column-fodder risk on a French-owned, security-gated nuclear account.
north_star_verdict.math: 162 days to close from 2026-07-06 (close 2026-12-15). Deal created 2026-04-23, 74 days old, all of it in Formal Evaluation since the 2026-05-28 stage move from Qualified. The RFP is in flight: the submission deadline was pushed 5 Jun to 26 Jun (Next Step 2026-05-27) and again to 17 Jul, with Orano sending additional clarifications on 2026-07-02, so the process is active, not dead. But the motion is one-directional RFP paperwork: no discovery session, no demo, no NDA executed, and no buyer-side stakeholder mapped in 74 days. To close by 15 Dec the deal must run shortlist, demos, workshops, commercials, ROI, EB approval, and contracting in ~5 months from a zero-relationship base, which is optimistic. Forecast Pipeline is correct.
evidence: RFP submission is live and was extended to 17 Jul with additional clarifications received on 2026-07-02 (SF Task 2026-07-02), so the buyer is engaged on the process
74 days in Formal Evaluation with no discovery session, no demo, and no NDA executed despite these being open next steps since 2026-05-12 (SF Next Step)
Zero buyer-side OpportunityContactRoles; Chris Chovin is the only named contact and reads as an RFP/procurement coordinator, role and influence unconfirmed
No Economic Buyer or Decision Maker engaged; the April 23 discussion put decision authority behind the French parent's data-security governance (AIS rationale, SF Description)
No champion advocating internally and no competitive intel: incumbent, vendor count, and evaluation criteria all unknown

metrics.narrative: On the 23 April discussion with Zycus, Chris Chovin described Orano's current state as manual Excel and Outlook workflows and a desire for a blank-slate source-to-contract platform to replace outdated tools (SF AIS rationale, Description). That establishes a qualitative digitisation pain, but no quantified impact has been captured anywhere: no spend under management, cycle-time, headcount, compliance-risk, or savings figures are on record, and no MEDDPICC value fields are populated. Quantifying the business case with a named finance or procurement sponsor is a ranked move; until then the value story is scope-inferred (full S2P suite) rather than buyer-confirmed.
sources: SF AIS_Why (23 Apr call, Chris Chovin)

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is named in any source: there are zero OpportunityContactRoles, no EB MEDDPICC fields populated, and Chris Chovin (our only contact) reads as an RFP/procurement coordinator rather than budget authority. The one directional clue is the 23 April call, where Chris Chovin flagged that data-security and adoption constraints flow down from Orano's French parent and nuclear regulation, which suggests real decision and sign-off authority sits with the French ownership's security/IT governance, not the Canadian procurement desk we are talking to. Identifying and reaching that budget owner is a top move before the RFP scoring locks positioning.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation is a formal RFP, and the 23 April call surfaced the criteria that will actually decide it: nuclear-grade data security, compliance with French-parent governance, and ease/speed of adoption given a workforce moving off Excel and Outlook (SF AIS rationale). Breadth also matters, given the full S2P scope requested (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin). These criteria are inferred from a single early call and the RFP scope rather than confirmed line-by-line against the RFP scoring sheet; obtaining the weighted RFP criteria from Chris Chovin would convert this from partial to confirmed.
sources: SF AIS_Why (23 Apr call, Chris Chovin)

decision_process.narrative: Orano is running a structured RFP: intent-to-respond was due 20 May, and the submission deadline has been formally extended twice (5 Jun to 26 Jun per Chris Chovin's 27 May email, then to 17 Jul with additional clarifications issued on 2 Jul). That cadence tells us there is a governed evaluation with a real timeline, managed through Chris Chovin. What is missing is the shape after submission: no shortlist criteria, demo/workshop plan, or approval chain is documented, and the French-parent security gating means final approval likely routes through ownership governance we have not accessed. Mapping the post-submission stages is the process move.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process is early and largely dark. The only mechanic visible is the NDA: 'Execute NDA for formal engagement' has been an open next step since 12 May and is still not executed 74 days in, which blocks deeper discovery, roadmap and technical sharing. No MSA, Order Form, SOW, legal-review, InfoSec-questionnaire, or procurement-channel detail is on record, and Current_Contract_Expiration__c is empty so there is no contractual forcing function. Given nuclear-sector security, expect a heavy InfoSec/DPA track once engaged; getting the NDA signed and the RFP's security requirements in writing is what would create visibility here.

identify_pain.narrative: The confirmed pain from the 23 April discussion is operational: Orano runs sourcing and contracting on manual Excel and Outlook and wants a modern, unified source-to-contract platform (SF AIS rationale). Chris Chovin also articulated constraint-pain: heavy data-security requirements and slow adoption risk stemming from French ownership and nuclear regulation, which is as much a risk to solve as a benefit to sell. The pain owner is not yet named beyond Chris Chovin, and the financial/operational cost of the status quo is unquantified. This is genuine, but confirmed by one early call rather than multi-threaded discovery.
sources: SF AIS_Why (23 Apr call, Chris Chovin)

champion.narrative: No champion exists. Chris Chovin is the only named contact and the sole evidence of him is transactional: emailing RFP deadline extensions and clarifications (Next Step 2026-05-27, Task 2026-07-02). There is no advocacy signal, no introductions to other stakeholders, no coaching on criteria or competitors, and no evidence he is a business sponsor rather than a procurement coordinator. In an RFP with no relationship, the absence of a champion is the single biggest structural risk, because our Merlin/security differentiation will not be argued for us inside the committee. Earning a coach who will map the committee is the priority.

competition.narrative: The competitive picture is entirely dark: Competitors__c and the free-text competition fields are empty, no incumbent tool is named beyond generic Excel/Outlook manual workflows, and there is no read on how many vendors are in the RFP. We do not know whether this is a displacement of a legacy suite, a consolidation of point tools, or a net-new build, which means we cannot yet aim the Zycus wedge (unified S2P plus nuclear-grade security). Because the shortlist is usually named once in early discovery, extracting the vendor field and incumbent from Chris Chovin is a required move before we can position to win rather than merely comply.
sources: SF Next Step / Tasks (no competitor named)

deal_movement.summary: The deal is an active but thin RFP: real process motion (two deadline extensions, fresh clarifications on 2 Jul) against a flat relationship (single contact, no discovery, no NDA). Last genuine buyer touch is Orano's 2 Jul RFP clarifications and deadline push to 17 Jul.

items.change: Orano extended the RFP submission deadline to 17 Jul and sent additional responses and clarifications that do not materially change the Zycus response
RFP submission deadline extended from 5 Jun to 26 Jun per Chris Chovin email; Zycus working submission with weekly check-ins
Stage advanced from Qualified to Formal Evaluation
Deal reassigned to Mike Flowers and moved Initial Interest to Qualified
Discovery discussion with Zycus: Chris Chovin described a blank-slate S2C need to replace Excel/Outlook under French-parent security constraints

competitive_position.summary: Competitive landscape is completely dark. No competitor, incumbent, or RFP vendor count is named in any Salesforce field, next step, task, or call, so we cannot yet tell whether this is a displacement, a consolidation of manual point tools, or a net-new build. The nearest thing to an incumbent is Orano's manual Excel/Outlook state (23 Apr call). The single most important competitive action is not ranking rivals but discovering them: extract the vendor shortlist and any incumbent from Chris Chovin before RFP scoring, or we risk positioning blind.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Orano's stated expectations center on nuclear-grade data security, French-parent compliance, and low-friction adoption off manual tools; Zycus fits the S2P breadth but the security/governance bar is unproven and our AI story is a poor lead here.

best_practice_check.summary: This is a live formal RFP where Zycus holds zero relationship. Winning depends on breaking single-threading, positioning on security over AI, and getting a read on the competitive field before scoring locks.
flags: Break single-threading now: Chris Chovin is a process coordinator, not a sponsor, so get to the S2P business owner and the French-parent IT/security lead before RFP scoring.
Win on security and adoption, not AI: lead the response with nuclear-grade data-security and change-management support given the French-ownership constraints; hold Merlin as a later proof point.
Close the competitive blind spot: find out who else is bidding and whether a legacy tool is being displaced so the wedge can be aimed rather than generic.

items.name: Chris Chovin

champion_strength.summary: No champion identified or developed. Chris Chovin is the only named contact and his role reads as RFP/procurement coordination, with no evidence of internal advocacy, introductions, or criteria coaching. In an RFP with zero relationship, the missing champion is the single biggest structural risk to the win.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin is in the product scope but AI is the wrong lead for this buyer: the 23 April call recorded zero AI interest and explicit data-security/adoption caution from French ownership and nuclear regulation. Positioning strength is low here by design; leading with AI would hurt, so keep Merlin as a Phase 2 proof point behind security and unified S2P.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS is 4/Moderate, but the rationale is explicit that the buyer showed no AI appetite: no mention of Merlin, ANA, agentic, GenAI, or intake, and security gating from the French nuclear parent constrains AI adoption. Treat this as security-first, not AI-led.

No economic buyer or executive sponsor engaged 74 days in; we have no access to power.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: We are responding to Orano's formal S2P RFP (submission due 17 Jul) but with no champion, no economic buyer, and a single procurement coordinator as our only contact, a serious column-fodder risk on a French-owned, security-gated nuclear account.

win_position.text: Win position is low: we are one of several vendors answering Orano's formal RFP with no relationship inside the committee, with Chris Chovin, an RFP coordinator, as our only contact (Next Step 27 May; discovery call 23 Apr).
No economic buyer or budget owner is engaged and decision authority appears to sit with the French parent's security governance, which we have not reached (23 Apr call, AIS rationale).
No champion is coaching us on criteria or the competitor set, so our security and Merlin differentiation risks going unheard on an AI-cautious nuclear account (SF Contact Roles, zero roles).

deal_momentum.text: The RFP is genuinely live: Orano extended the deadline twice (5 Jun to 26 Jun to 17 Jul) and sent additional clarifications on 2 Jul, so the process is active (Task 2026-07-02).
But the motion is one-directional RFP paperwork: no discovery session, no demo, and no NDA executed despite these being open since 12 May (SF Next Step).

customer_commitment.text: Commitment is minimal: Orano shared an RFP and set submission dates but made no Zycus-specific ask, granted no discovery access, and named no sponsor (Next Step 27 May).

deal_risk.text: The core risk is being column fodder on a compliance RFP: single-threaded to a coordinator, no champion, no economic buyer, and no competitive intel on a 150k, security-constrained nuclear account.

champion.evidence: No champion; only Chris Chovin, an RFP coordinator (Next Step 27 May)

critical_signals.text: No competitor or incumbent has surfaced yet; we are answering a formal RFP with no read on the field or whether a legacy tool is being displaced.
Chris Chovin, a procurement/contracts coordinator, is our only contact and we have never reached a business sponsor or the budget owner at the French parent.
The last real exchange was Orano's 2 Jul RFP clarifications and a deadline push to 17 Jul, active process but still paperwork, not a discovery conversation.
The deal is 150k but with no budget owner engaged and no ROI discussion, the price cannot yet be justified or defended.
